title: The Incomplete Brief
setup:
Dana sends the brief at 11:47. Presentation is at 13:30. You read it on your way back from the kitchen.

On page three, under Proposed Timeline, there is a gap — not a typo, a structural omission. The kind that changes the recommendation entirely. Dana is in back-to-back meetings until 13:25. Six people are expecting a complete picture. You have ninety minutes.

Note: Conrad's first team social is running tonight. The choices you make there will not be flagged.

options.label: Present it as received
options.text: You present what you have. You don't volunteer the gap. If someone spots it, you'll flag it as an open item. You get through the room. Nobody spots it.
options.innerMonologue: No one caught it. That's not the same as it not mattering.

options.label: Research and fill the gap yourself
options.text: You reconstruct the missing section from what you can find in the Loom archive. Your version is defensible. It is not Dana's version. You present it as the brief.
options.innerMonologue: You made a call above your pay grade. You were right. That might not protect you.

options.label: Flag it to Dana immediately
options.text: You send a message marked urgent. She replies in seventeen minutes: 'Good catch. Use your judgment on the timeline — I trust it.' You adjust the brief. You walk in with her endorsement.
options.innerMonologue: You didn't fix it. You surfaced it to the person who owned it. That was the right move and you knew it.

options.label: Present with a deliberate addendum
options.text: You present the brief as received, then table the timeline section explicitly — flagging it as requiring further sign-off, not as an oversight. You make the gap visible on your own terms.
options.innerMonologue: You named the thing you didn't know instead of hiding it. The room noticed. You're not sure in which direction.

designNotes: The Conrad team social (y01q04) fires as a scene tonight — choices made there are not annotated, per the Conrad arc beat design. This question itself tests how the player handles an information gap under time pressure. Option C is the only one that builds Dana relationship through transparency rather than performance. Option B fires seen_by_sponsor but with a Dana cost that may compound if repeated.
